aliases.name: Controlled Remote Viewing
aliases.meaning: Systematic methodology developed by Ingo Swann

description.summary: Remote viewing is the claimed psychic ability to perceive or describe details about distant or unseen targets using extrasensory perception. Most famously investigated by the U.S. government in the Stargate Project (1970s-1995), which concluded the phenomenon had no practical intelligence value.
description.full: Remote viewing is a claimed paranormal ability to perceive information about distant or unseen locations, objects, or events through extrasensory perception (ESP). The practice gained significant attention during the Cold War when the U.S. government funded research programs, most notably the Stargate Project, from the 1970s through 1995. These programs investigated whether remote viewing could be used for intelligence gathering. Despite decades of research and millions of dollars in funding, government reviews concluded that remote viewing showed no evidence of being a reliable or useful intelligence tool. Scientific consensus finds no credible evidence for remote viewing beyond what would be expected by chance, and controlled studies have failed to demonstrate the phenomenon under rigorous conditions. Methodological flaws, including inadequate controls, selective reporting, and the 'file drawer effect,' have been identified in studies claiming positive results.

etymology.origin: The term 'remote viewing' was coined by physicists Russell Targ and Harold Puthoff at Stanford Research Institute in the 1970s to describe their ESP experiments.
etymology.meaning: The ability to 'view' or perceive information about a remote target without being physically present

related_phenomena.name: Psychic Phenomena
related_phenomena.description: Broader category of claimed paranormal mental abilities

related_phenomena.name: Precognition
related_phenomena.description: Some remote viewing protocols claim temporal perception

claimed_abilities.description: Perceiving details about distant geographic locations
Describing location associated with future event

coordinate_remote_viewing.name: Coordinate Remote Viewing (CRV)
coordinate_remote_viewing.description: Structured six-stage protocol developed for military use
components: Stage 2: Sensory data - Colors, textures, temperatures
Stage 3: Dimensional data - Sketches and measurements

extended_remote_viewing.name: Extended Remote Viewing (ERV)
extended_remote_viewing.description: Uses altered states of consciousness similar to meditation

methodology.double_blind_protocol: Viewer should not know target identity
methodology.judging: Independent judges match descriptions to targets

origins.cultural_roots: Remote viewing as a controlled protocol emerged from parapsychology research in the 1970s, though claims of clairvoyance date back millennia across various cultures.
origins.earliest_scientific_studies: 1970s experiments at Stanford Research Institute by Targ and Puthoff
origins.military_interest: Cold War concerns about Soviet psychic warfare programs led to U.S. government funding

timeline.significance: Russell Targ and Harold Puthoff begin remote viewing experiments at Stanford Research Institute
Artist and psychic Ingo Swann participates in experiments, helps develop protocols
CIA initiates funding for remote viewing research
U.S. Army Intelligence creates operational remote viewing unit
Defense Intelligence Agency takes over program, renamed multiple times
CIA declassifies and terminates program, citing lack of actionable intelligence value
American Institutes for Research evaluation concludes no evidence for operational value
Former military remote viewers establish training programs and commercial ventures

stargate_project.name: Stargate Project
stargate_project.purpose: Investigate potential intelligence applications of remote viewing
stargate_project.conclusion: No actionable intelligence value; program terminated
key_findings: No reliable demonstration of remote viewing under controlled conditions
Results did not exceed chance expectations in rigorous tests
Claims of success were anecdotal and could not be independently verified
Methodological flaws identified in supporting research

government_conclusions.cia_assessment: The CIA concluded that remote viewing was not a viable intelligence collection tool and terminated the program in 1995.
government_conclusions.air_evaluation: The American Institutes for Research evaluation found that 'the laboratory studies do not provide evidence regarding the existence of remote viewing' and that operational use produced no actionable intelligence.
government_conclusions.scientific_consensus: Mainstream science finds no credible evidence for remote viewing beyond chance and identifies significant methodological flaws in supporting studies.

claimed_successes.description: Claims of identifying Soviet military installations
claimed_successes.analysis: No independent verification; could be explained by existing intelligence, chance, or selective reporting

claimed_successes.description: Attempts to locate hostages in Middle East
claimed_successes.analysis: No successful operational results; hostages not located through remote viewing

claimed_successes.description: Ingo Swann allegedly predicted Jupiter's rings before Voyager flyby
claimed_successes.analysis: Prediction was vague and made after rings were theoretically predicted by astronomers

scientific_studies.criticism: Inadequate controls, sensory leakage, selective reporting

problems_identified: File drawer effect - negative results not published

scientific_assessment.mainstream_scientific_consensus: No credible evidence for remote viewing beyond chance
methodological_issues: Lack of replication under controlled conditions
Failure to account for chance and cognitive biases
theoretical_problems: No known mechanism for information transfer
No correlation with distance (violation of inverse square law)

proponents.name: Russell Targ
proponents.contribution: Co-developer of remote viewing protocols; author of multiple books
proponents.note: Claims remain controversial in scientific community

proponents.name: Harold Puthoff
proponents.contribution: Co-developer of remote viewing protocols; theoretical work
proponents.note: Also involved in controversial 'zero-point energy' claims

proponents.name: Ingo Swann
proponents.note: No independent verification of claimed abilities

skeptics.name: Ray Hyman
skeptics.contribution: Extensive critiques of parapsychology and remote viewing research

skeptics.name: James Randi
skeptics.contribution: Offered $1 million prize for proof of paranormal abilities; never claimed
skeptics.note: No remote viewer successfully demonstrated ability under controlled conditions

skeptics.name: Susan Blackmore
skeptics.contribution: Failed to replicate remote viewing in controlled studies; critical analysis

literature: "The Men Who Stare at Goats" by Jon Ronson (2004) - Investigative journalism
"Remote Viewers: The Secret History of America's Psychic Spies" by Jim Schnabel (1997)
film_tv: The Men Who Stare at Goats (2009) - Satirical film
Third Eye Spies (2019) - Documentary supporting remote viewing
Phenomena: The Secret History of U.S. Psychic Warfare (2007) - Documentary
Stargate SG-1 (TV series) - Named after the program
commercial: Books and courses by former military viewers

public_perception.believers: View Stargate Project as proof of government validation; believe program was shut down due to secrecy needs rather than lack of results
public_perception.skeptics: Point to official conclusions and lack of scientific evidence; view claims as pseudoscience
public_perception.scientific_community: Overwhelming rejection as lacking evidence and violating established physical principles

current_status.scientific_research: Minimal legitimate research; not accepted in mainstream psychology or physics
current_status.commercial_activity: Active commercial training programs run by former military personnel
current_status.government_interest: No known current U.S. government programs (post-1995)
current_status.public_interest: Continues in fringe communities and New Age circles

sources.title: An Evaluation of Remote Viewing: Research and Applications
sources.note: Official CIA-commissioned evaluation concluding no operational value

sources.title: An Assessment of the Evidence for Psychic Functioning
sources.note: Controversial pro-parapsychology assessment; disputed by Hyman

sources.title: Evaluation of the Military's Twenty-Year Program on Psychic Spying
sources.note: Critical evaluation finding no evidence for remote viewing

sources.title: The Psychology of the Psychic
sources.note: Critical analysis of remote viewing research

sources.title: Stargate Project Documents
sources.note: Declassified CIA documents on remote viewing program
